### 3.1.4 Atomic Mass

The *atomic mass* (often referred to as *atomic weight*) of an element is the average mass of all its naturally occurring isotopes. The standard unit for atomic mass (*atomic mass unit*, *abbreviated as ‘amu’*) is the *dalton*, which is symbolized as Da. The mass of a neutron is 1.008 Da, the mass of a proton is 1.007 Da and the mass of an electron is 0.0005 Da. In its purest sense, the Da can be defined as one-twelfth of the mass of a carbon 12C atom and therefore equates to the extremely small mass of 1.66 × 10−24 g! When rounded to the nearest whole number, the atomic mass of an element typically coincides with the mass number of the predominant isotope of that element (see Figure 3.2).

## Definition

The *atomic mass* (often referred to as *atomic weight*) of an element is the average mass of all its naturally occurring isotopes. The standard unit is the *atomic mass unit* (amu), known as the *dalton* (symbol: Da), defined as one-twelfth of the mass of a carbon-12 (¹²C) atom, equating to approximately 1.66 × 10⁻²⁴ g.

## Core Principles

- Atomic mass is computed as the weighted average mass across all naturally occurring isotopes of an element.
- The dalton (Da) is operationally defined as 1/12 of the mass of a ¹²C atom.
- Approximate subatomic particle masses in daltons:
  - Neutron: 1.008 Da
  - Proton: 1.007 Da
  - Electron: 0.0005 Da
- When rounded to the nearest whole number, the atomic mass of an element typically coincides with the mass number of its predominant (most abundant) isotope.

## Sub-concepts

- **Atomic mass unit (amu) / Dalton (Da):** Standard unit of atomic mass.
- **Isotopic averaging:** Atomic mass reflects the natural abundance-weighted mean of isotope masses.
- **Mass number vs. atomic mass:** Mass number is an integer count of nucleons; atomic mass is a measured (typically non-integer) average that often rounds to the mass number of the dominant isotope.

## Practical Applications

- Used to convert between mass and moles for elements and compounds in stoichiometric and biochemical calculations.
- Provides the basis for molecular weight determination of biomolecules relevant to sport and exercise biochemistry.
